codeSnapshot.label: efficientnet.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torchvision.models as models
import torch

# Load EfficientNet variants
for name, model_fn in [
    ("EfficientNet-B0", models.efficientnet_b0),
    ("EfficientNet-B3", models.efficientnet_b3),
    ("EfficientNet-B7", models.efficientnet_b7),
]:
    model = model_fn(weights=None)
    params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()) / 1e6
    # Input resolution: B0=224, B3=300, B7=600
    print(f"{name}: {params:.1f}M parameters")
# EfficientNet-B0:  5.3M
# EfficientNet-B3:  12.2M
# EfficientNet-B7:  66.3M

# --- Transfer learning with EfficientNet-B0 ---
from torchvision.models import EfficientNet_B0_Weights
import torch.nn as nn

model = models.efficientnet_b0(weights=EfficientNet_B0_Weights.DEFAULT)

# Freeze all backbone layers
for param in model.features.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Replace classifier head for 10-class task
num_features = model.classifier[1].in_features  # 1280
model.classifier = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Dropout(p=0.2, inplace=True),
    nn.Linear(num_features, 10)
)

# Only classifier parameters are trainable
trained_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"Trainable params (head only): {trained_params:,}")

eli3: Previous networks got better by adding more layers (deeper), adding more filters (wider), or using higher resolution images. EfficientNet asked: what if you do all three at the same time in the right proportions? Instead of guessing, they used a mathematical formula to find the best balance.

They found that if you have a budget to double computational cost, you should scale depth by 1.2×, width by 1.1×, and resolution by 1.15× simultaneously. Doing just one of these wastes your budget; doing all three in harmony gives you much better accuracy per FLOP.

The result was a family of 8 models (B0 through B7), where each is about 2× the compute of the previous but significantly more accurate. EfficientNet-B7 achieved state-of-the-art ImageNet accuracy with 8.4× fewer parameters and 6.1× fewer FLOPs than the previous best (GPipe).

whenWhy.title: Resource-constrained deployment (mobile, edge)
whenWhy.body: EfficientNet-B0 achieves ~77.1% top-1 ImageNet accuracy with only 5.3M parameters and ~0.39 GFLOPs — comparable to ResNet-50 (~76.0% accuracy) at 8× fewer FLOPs. EfficientNet-Lite variants remove squeeze-and-excitation and use ReLU6 to maximise hardware efficiency on fixed-point inference. For mobile deployment (phones, embedded devices with INT8 accelerators), EfficientNet models offer the best accuracy-per-FLOP tradeoff in the CNN family as of 2024.

whenWhy.title: Transfer learning baseline for new visual tasks
whenWhy.body: EfficientNet-B0 to B3 pretrained on ImageNet are standard transfer learning starting points for tasks with limited labeled data (medical imaging, satellite imagery, industrial defect detection). The compound scaling ensures these models are not over-engineered for ImageNet and generalise well. Fine-tuning the full network (lower LR for earlier layers) typically outperforms feature extraction, but even linear probing with frozen EfficientNet-B0 features gives competitive results.

whenWhy.title: NAS-designed MBConv blocks
whenWhy.body: EfficientNet's baseline (B0) was found by Neural Architecture Search (NAS) using a multi-objective reward based on accuracy and FLOPs. The search discovered MBConv (Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution) blocks: expand channels with 1×1, apply 3×3 or 5×5 depthwise conv, squeeze with 1×1, add Squeeze-and-Excitation. This block design, originally from MobileNetV2, proved to be the most FLOP-efficient building block. Understanding MBConv blocks explains why EfficientNet achieves better accuracy per FLOP than ResNet's standard bottleneck blocks.

deepDiveIntro: Tan & Le (2019) proposed compound scaling: rather than picking one dimension to enlarge, scale depth, width, and image resolution simultaneously using a single coefficient. The ratios between the three dimensions were found by a small grid search on the baseline model, constrained so that doubling the coefficient approximately doubles the total compute. This constraint means depth, width, and resolution grow in a fixed, balanced proportion with each step up the B0–B7 ladder, giving a predictable accuracy-versus-compute tradeoff. [1]

The baseline B0 was designed by Neural Architecture Search rather than by hand. It uses inverted bottleneck blocks: channels are first expanded (making the filter wider), then spatial features are extracted with a depthwise convolution (one filter per channel), then compressed back down. A small channel-attention module (Squeeze-and-Excitation) recalibrates each channel's importance based on the global image content, adding about 1% accuracy at negligible compute cost. [2]

deepDive.heading: Compound Scaling Formula
deepDive.body: Each unit increase in the compound coefficient approximately doubles the total compute because the chosen ratios satisfy a constraint: when you multiply the depth, width-squared, and resolution-squared factors together you get roughly 2. Depth scales compute linearly, while width and resolution each scale compute quadratically (because both input and output channels grow, and both spatial dimensions grow). The key insight from the paper is that previous ad-hoc scaling — for example, simply doubling the depth — wastes compute budget that could have been better spent on balanced growth across all three dimensions. [1]

deepDive.heading: MBConv Block: Inverted Residual + SE
deepDive.body: An MBConv block first expands the channel count by a factor of 6 using a 1x1 convolution, then applies a depthwise convolution (one separate filter per channel, which is far cheaper than standard convolutions), then compresses back to the original channel count. Squeeze-and-Excitation adds a small learned attention vector: it pools each feature map to a single number, runs a two-layer network to produce a channel importance score between 0 and 1, and multiplies each feature map by its score. The residual skip connection around the whole block is applied when input and output shapes match. [2]

deepDive.heading: EfficientNet vs ResNet: Accuracy-Efficiency Tradeoff
deepDive.body: EfficientNet-B0 matches ResNet-50's ImageNet accuracy at 8x fewer parameters and 10x fewer floating-point operations. EfficientNet-B3 exceeds ResNet-152 by more than 5 percentage points with 5x fewer parameters and 6x fewer operations. The gains come from three sources: the more efficient MBConv building block with channel attention, the Swish non-linearity instead of ReLU, and the balanced compound scaling. Vision Transformers and ConvNeXt have since surpassed EfficientNet at the largest scales, but it remains one of the best CNN architectures for mid-range compute budgets. [1]

deepDive.heading: EfficientNetV2 and Improved Training
deepDive.body: The original EfficientNet's large-resolution variants (B7 uses 600×600 inputs) train very slowly, and their depthwise convolutions are poorly utilised on modern GPU and TPU hardware. EfficientNetV2 addresses both problems: it replaces depthwise convolutions in the early layers with standard 3×3 convolutions (called Fused-MBConv), which are better suited to hardware accelerators, and uses progressive learning — starting training with small images and weak augmentation, then increasing both gradually. EfficientNetV2-M achieves 85% ImageNet accuracy while training 11x faster than EfficientNet-B7. [2]

takeaways: Compound scaling: d=α^φ, w=β^φ, r=γ^φ with α·β²·γ²≈2; balanced scaling outperforms scaling any single dimension. [1]
MBConv block: 1×1 expand → depthwise conv → Squeeze-and-Excitation → 1×1 project; FLOP-efficient building block. [2]
EfficientNet-B0 matches ResNet-50 accuracy with 8× fewer parameters and 10× fewer FLOPs. [1]
SE modules reweight channels via a learned attention vector — ~1% top-1 accuracy gain at negligible FLOP cost. [2]
EfficientNetV2 uses Fused-MBConv for early stages and progressive training, achieving 11× faster training than B7. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why doesn't compound scaling guarantee optimal performance — what are its limitations?
edgeCases.a: Compound scaling has several limitations: (1) It scales a fixed baseline architecture — the scaling coefficients are optimal for MBConv blocks but not necessarily for other architectures (ResNets, ViTs). (2) The constraint α·β²·γ²≈2 is an approximation; actual FLOP scaling deviates at extreme φ due to fixed-cost components (stem, head). (3) The grid search for α,β,γ is done at φ=1, assuming the optimal coefficients are constant across all scales — this may not hold at very large scales. (4) Resolution scaling becomes expensive: B7 at 600×600 uses 7.2× more spatial pixels than B0 at 224×224, requiring very large batches or gradient checkpointing. EfficientNetV2 addressed the resolution bottleneck with progressive training and Fused-MBConv.

edgeCases.q: How does Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) work and why does it help?
edgeCases.a: SE adds a channel attention mechanism: (1) Squeeze — Global Average Pool each of the C feature maps to a single number, producing a C-dimensional descriptor of 'what is in this image'. (2) Excitation — pass through a small FC(C/r) → ReLU → FC(C) → Sigmoid network, producing a C-dimensional vector of channel importance weights in (0,1). (3) Scale — multiply each feature map channel by its importance weight. This allows the network to adaptively recalibrate channel responses: focus on informative channels (e.g., the 'wheel detector' channel when the input contains a car) and suppress less informative ones. SE adds 2×C×C/r+1 parameters per block (r=4 typically), negligible relative to the conv parameters, but provides consistent +0.5–1.5% ImageNet top-1 accuracy.

sources.label: Tan & Le (2019) — EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks, ICML 2019 arXiv:1905.11946
Tan & Le (2021) — EfficientNetV2: Smaller Models and Faster Training, ICML 2021 arXiv:2104.00298
